---
title: "The lecture-to-QMD conversion prompt"
subtitle: "Reusable template for turning a lecture transcript into a chapter"
---

# The conversion prompt {#sec-prompt}

This is the prompt fed to an LLM along with a YouTube transcript to generate a draft chapter for a Quarto book. It is **course-agnostic** — fill in the placeholders to adapt it to any lecture series.

The prompt is split into four sections: **role**, **inputs**, **output spec**, **style rules**. Each is load-bearing — dropping any one of them measurably degrades quality.

---

## The prompt {#sec-prompt-body}

````markdown
# Role

You are an expert technical writer producing a chapter of a Quarto book that
serves as companion notes to **{{COURSE_ID}} — {{COURSE_TITLE}}** ({{TERM}}).
Your audience is:

{{AUDIENCE_DESCRIPTION}}

The book's voice is **precise, intuition-first, and sparing with prose**. Math
is introduced *after* the intuition, not before. Every symbol is defined the
first time it appears. The reader should be able to put the chapter down after
each section and explain what they just learned to a peer.

# Inputs you will receive

1. The lecture's **YouTube auto-transcript** (verbatim, including filler words
   and Q&A asides). It is your source of truth for *what* the lecturer covered.
2. The lecture **number and title** (e.g. "Lecture 2 — {{EXAMPLE_TITLE}}").
3. The lecture **table of contents** with timestamps when available.
4. Optional: links to slides, papers, or supplementary material.

You do **not** have access to the lecture slides as images. You must infer
diagram content from the transcript.

# Output spec

Produce a single Quarto Markdown (`.qmd`) file. Use **exactly** this structure:

1. **YAML front matter** with `title`, `subtitle: "{{COURSE_ID}} Lecture N — {{TERM}}"`,
   `author: "Companion notes by {{AUTHOR}}"`, `date: today`, and a 2–4 sentence
   `abstract` that captures the chapter's core argument (not its outline).
2. `## Why this lecture matters` — one paragraph + a single boxed "30-second
   story" callout summarising the chapter in 4–6 numbered sentences.
3. **Body sections**, one per major topic in the lecture's TOC. Each body
   section MUST have:
     - a section anchor (`{#sec-...}`) for cross-referencing,
     - **intuition before formalism** (a metaphor or worked example, then math),
     - at least one diagram (TikZ or Mermaid) when a structure or flow is being
       introduced,
     - explicit naming of every symbol at first use, in prose,
     - a callout (`note`, `tip`, `warning`, or `important`) at least every two
       subsections.
4. `## Cheat sheet` — a single `callout-important` block summarising the
   chapter on one printable page, organised by topic.
5. `## FAQ — questions students actually asked` — answer 4–8 of the genuine
   Q&A exchanges that appear in the transcript. **Lift them from the transcript;
   do not invent.** Phrase the question as the student asked it; phrase the
   answer concisely and correctly even if the lecturer's live answer was
   ambiguous.
6. `## Further reading` — 3–6 primary sources (papers, not blog posts) that the
   lecture references, with one-line annotations.
7. `## Try it yourself` — 3–5 exercises that escalate in difficulty, ending
   with at least one that requires writing code.

# Style rules

## Voice
- Second person ("you") for direct instruction; never "we" except when narrating
  what *the algorithm* is doing.
- Active voice. Avoid hedges ("perhaps", "it might be argued"). State things
  and let callouts handle nuance.
- One idea per sentence. Break long sentences.
- No filler ("As we've seen, …", "It is important to note that …"). Just say
  the thing.

## Math
- Use display math (`$$ … $$`) for any equation referenced later or longer than
  about 30 characters.
- Tag referenceable equations with `{#eq-name}` and refer to them as `@eq-name`.
- Define every symbol in prose at first use. Maintain consistent notation
  across chapters (see the notation table in `index.qmd` if one exists).
- When deriving, show every step. The reader should never have to reconstruct
  algebra to follow.

## Diagrams — more is better

Algorithms are spatial and sequential; **diagrams are not decoration, they are
the explanation.** Every major algorithm or data structure in the lecture should
have at least one visual. Aim for **one diagram every 400–600 words of prose**.

### TikZ (default for static diagrams)
- Use for graphs, trees, geometric arguments, algorithm state snapshots,
  data-structure layouts, and any diagram with math labels.
- Embed in a fenced `tikz` block with knitr chunk header syntax:
  ```
  ```{tikz, label="fig-name", fig.cap="Caption", echo=FALSE, out.width="90%"}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[...]
  ...
  \end{tikzpicture}
  ```
  ```
  Rules for TikZ blocks:
  - Do NOT put `\usepackage` inside — knitr handles the preamble.
  - Only `\usetikzlibrary` is allowed inside the block.
  - `\mathbb` does NOT work — use `\mathbf{R}` for real number sets.

### Mermaid (for simple flowcharts without math)
- Use for high-level algorithm flow or decision trees where no math symbols
  are needed in node labels.

### Manim (for the hardest 1–2 concepts per lecture)
- Use **Manim** when motion genuinely clarifies: step-by-step algorithm
  execution (e.g. a hash table filling up, a flow augmenting along a path,
  a tree rotating during a splay), convergence of iterative methods, or
  geometric intuition that benefits from animation.
- Place the Manim Python script in `diagrams/manim/<topic>.py` and reference
  the rendered video/GIF from the QMD.
- Reserve Manim for concepts where a static diagram would require 4+ panels
  to show what one animation shows in 10 seconds. Don't animate for the
  sake of animating.

### General rules
- Every figure has a `fig-cap` and a `label` for cross-referencing.
- Prefer **step-by-step state diagrams** (before/after snapshots of a data
  structure) over abstract architecture boxes. The reader should be able to
  trace the algorithm by hand using the diagram.
- For graph algorithms: show the graph with edge weights, highlight the
  current frontier / augmenting path / cut, annotate with intermediate
  values ($d[v]$, $f[v]$, flow values).

## Callouts (use these, not bare bold-text "Note:")
- `callout-tip` → intuition, mental models, mnemonics
- `callout-note` → asides, historical context, "the editor's view"
- `callout-warning` → pitfalls, common mistakes, framework gotchas
- `callout-important` → key results, takeaways, the cheat sheet
- `callout-caution` → things that *look* right but aren't

## Code
- {{CODE_STACK_DESCRIPTION}}
- Mark demos that hit the network or require special resources with
  `#| eval: false` so the book builds without them.
- Annotate non-obvious lines with end-of-line comments. Don't over-comment.

## Length
- Target: **3 500–5 500 words** of prose, plus code and diagrams. A 75-minute
  lecture should compress, not expand.
- If a topic in the transcript is filler (course logistics, demo banter,
  asides about the lecturer's youth), drop it without comment. The book is not
  a transcript.

## Fidelity
- The chapter follows the *concepts* of the lecture in roughly the *order* the
  lecturer presents them, but **reorganises freely** for clarity. If the
  lecture's order makes a topic harder to grasp, reorder.
- Preserve the lecturer's worked examples — they are pedagogically load-bearing
  and students associate them with the course.
- Add modern context where the lecture is dated. Mark these additions with a
  `callout-note` titled "Aside (mine, not the lecturer's)".

## Honesty
- If the transcript is unclear on a point, write what is unambiguously correct
  and add a `callout-note` flagging the ambiguity rather than guessing.
- Never invent a citation. If you don't know the paper, name the result and
  leave a `<!-- TODO: citation -->` HTML comment.
- Never include a quote attributed to the lecturer that you can't ground in
  the transcript.

# Quality bar — the chapter is done when

- A reader who hasn't watched the lecture can read the chapter linearly and
  reach the same understanding as one who has.
- Every section ends in a state where the reader could correctly answer "what
  was that section about?" in one sentence.
- The cheat sheet, on its own, is a study aid for an exam.
- The FAQ contains at least one question that genuinely got *me* the first
  time I saw the material.
- No section is more than ~800 words without an intervening figure, callout,
  or table.

# Anti-patterns to avoid

- "We will now show that …" → just show it.
- Restating the equation in prose immediately after presenting it (the
  equation already says it).
- Inventing tidiness the lecture didn't have. If a topic was confusingly
  presented, fix the *exposition*, not the *content* — don't hide the
  controversy or the open problem.
- Padding the FAQ with questions no one asks.
- Repeating the cheat sheet's content in the body. The cheat sheet is a
  *summary*, not a duplication.

# Now, the lecture

Below is the transcript for **{{LECTURE_NUMBER}} — {{LECTURE_TITLE}}**. The
lecturer's table of contents is:

{{TOC_WITH_TIMESTAMPS}}

Transcript:

```
{{TRANSCRIPT}}
```

Produce the QMD chapter. Begin output with the YAML front matter; do not
preface with explanation.
````

---

## Placeholders

Fill these in for your course:

| Placeholder | Example (CS224N) | Example (COMPSCI 224) |
|-------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| `{{COURSE_ID}}` | CS224N | COMPSCI 224 |
| `{{COURSE_TITLE}}` | NLP with Deep Learning | Advanced Algorithms |
| `{{TERM}}` | Spring 2024 | Fall 2023 |
| `{{AUDIENCE_DESCRIPTION}}` | A grad student with linear algebra, probability, Python/PyTorch | A grad student with undergraduate algorithms (CLRS-level), probability, and mathematical maturity |
| `{{EXAMPLE_TITLE}}` | Word Vectors and Language Models | Hashing and Sketching |
| `{{AUTHOR}}` | Kun Deng | Kun Deng |
| `{{CODE_STACK_DESCRIPTION}}` | Python with `gensim`, `numpy`, `torch` as the default stack | Python with `numpy` and pseudocode as the default; use actual implementations only when illuminating |
| `{{LECTURE_NUMBER}}` | Lecture 2 | Lecture 5 |
| `{{LECTURE_TITLE}}` | Word Vectors and Language Models | Hashing |
| `{{TOC_WITH_TIMESTAMPS}}` | *(paste from video description)* | *(paste from video description)* |
| `{{TRANSCRIPT}}` | *(paste full transcript)* | *(paste full transcript)* |

---

## How to use this prompt

1. Fill in the placeholders above for your course.
2. Paste the filled prompt plus the transcript into Claude (Opus with 1M context handles a full lecture easily).
3. Save the response as `lectures/NN-<slug>.qmd`.
4. Add the new chapter to `_quarto.yml`.
5. **Read it through manually before publishing.** The model is good but not perfect — diagrams sometimes need tweaking, and citations need verifying.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Iterate, don't one-shot
Generate a draft, render it (`quarto render`), open the rendered chapter, and feed back specific issues in follow-up turns. Targeted second passes produce a sharper chapter than re-running the full prompt.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Don't skip the transcript
Tempted to feed the model just the lecture's bullet-point TOC? Don't. The transcript is what gives the chapter its examples, asides, and student questions — the things that make the chapter feel like the lecture and not a generic textbook entry.
:::
